Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and work through things, but most weeks I'd just end up scrolling through the materials without really committing to anything. I'd maybe spend an hour here or there, usually late at night when I felt guilty about not doing more. It's not like I didn't have the time or resources—I just couldn't seem to make myself care enough to structure anything properly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, though not really tied to deadlines either. Some weeks I'd log in a few times, other weeks barely at all. I showed up to class almost every time, which is weird because that didn't translate into actual studying. I think I was just going through the motions, waiting for something to click that never really did.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while, or sometimes a peer would mention something was due. There wasn't any real urgency or curiosity driving me—it was more like a vague sense of obligation. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something, even if I didn't actually engage with anything meaningful once I was there.
Q4: Not really, no. There were a couple of weeks early on where I thought I'd make it a habit, but it never stuck. I'd open the LMS, look around, and then just... drift off to something else. It never became automatic for me the way it seems to for other students. I guess I just didn't find enough there to keep pulling me back.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful when I actually engaged with them, since I learn better from text than videos. Some of the discussion posts from other students helped me understand concepts I'd missed. But honestly, even the useful stuff felt like a chore most of the time. I'd read through something and then forget it almost immediately because I wasn't really invested.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the videos after a while—watched a bunch at the start but they felt long and I'd zone out. The discussion forums I barely touched because posting felt pointless when I wasn't sure what I wanted to say. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials. If it wasn't absolutely required, I probably didn't do it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to something I had to submit, or if I could see an immediate practical use. Abstract stuff or things that felt like busywork just made me tune out. The problem is, even when something was objectively useful, I often couldn't muster the energy to care. My motivation was just... not there.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, which I mostly did. Required ones at least had some external pressure, but even then I only completed about half of my assignments. The difference was guilt, I guess—I'd feel worse about skipping required stuff, but not enough to actually do it consistently. Everything kind of blurred together after a while.
Q9: Yeah, I'd sometimes download PDFs or readings and have them open on my laptop without being logged into the LMS. I'd highlight things, make notes in a separate document. I also used some external websites to look up concepts when the course materials didn't make sense. So there was some studying happening that wouldn't show up in any logs.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with my notes from lectures, trying to piece things together, or I'd be thinking through a concept while doing something else entirely. I'd read through downloaded materials on the bus or at home without being online. The LMS would show nothing for those hours, but I was technically engaging with the content—just not in a trackable way.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a PDF once, then work through it over several days without going back to the LMS. Sometimes I'd print things out because reading on paper feels different to me. The system would show one quick access, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. It's just not captured anywhere.
Q12: Around midway through the semester, things got really hard. I was feeling pretty sad and unmotivated, and the workload from all twelve courses started piling up. I'd show up to class but then go home and just... not do anything. The peer support sessions helped a bit, but they couldn't fix whatever was going on in my head. I just felt stuck.
Q13: The sheer number of courses I was taking definitely affected things. I was spread thin and couldn't prioritize properly. When I was tired or confused about something, I'd just close the LMS instead of pushing through. Competing responsibilities weren't really the issue—I had the resources and time. It was more internal, like a fog that made everything feel pointless.
Q14: All the time, honestly. I'd open modules, scroll through pages, maybe watch part of a video, but my brain wasn't absorbing anything. I was physically present but mentally checked out. It's frustrating because I know I was logged in and technically 'active,' but I couldn't tell you what I learned during those sessions. It was hollow engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed before I even started. Part of it was just not caring enough to push through the discomfort. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It became a cycle I couldn't break.
Q16: Deadlines would sometimes jolt me into action, but often too late to do quality work. I'd scramble at the last minute, submit something half-finished, or miss it entirely. The deadline pressure didn't motivate me the way it should have—it just added stress without actually changing my behavior much. I'd see the date approaching and feel paralyzed instead of productive.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. When I got comments on assignments, I'd read them but wouldn't know what to do with the information. It felt like the damage was already done. Maybe if feedback had come earlier or more frequently, it would have helped, but by the time I got it, I was already checked out mentally.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard—modules, deadlines, discussions. But having twelve courses meant I couldn't give any single one the attention it probably needed. The structure itself wasn't bad, but I wasn't in a state to take advantage of it. I needed something more to pull me in, and the format alone wasn't enough.
Q19: The flexibility was supposed to help, but for someone like me, it just meant more opportunities to procrastinate. Clearer, smaller checkpoints might have kept me on track. The discussion requirements felt arbitrary, and the videos being so long made them easy to skip. I think more interactive elements or accountability would have helped, but I'm not sure anything could have fixed my motivation issues.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I'd log in, click around, watch parts of videos, open documents—but then not submit the actual work. It's like I was performing engagement without committing to it. Maybe it's avoidance, or maybe it's hoping that exposure alone would be enough. It wasn't, obviously.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline or already knows the material, they wouldn't need to be on the LMS much. That's not really my situation though—my low activity matched my low performance. But I get that logs don't capture everything. Someone could be deeply engaged in ways the system can't see.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you stayed. But they can't show whether you were actually thinking or just staring at the screen. They miss offline studying, emotional state, real comprehension. For someone like me, the logs might show some activity, but they'd completely miss how disconnected I felt the whole time.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning—wanting to understand it, thinking about it outside of class, feeling some connection to the material. For me, I was barely engaged at all. I showed up physically, I logged in occasionally, but my mind and heart weren't in it. Real engagement is something I couldn't access this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad and unmotivated I felt throughout the course. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that were the only thing keeping me somewhat connected. It wouldn't show the internal struggle of wanting to do better but not being able to make myself act on it. The data shows what I did, not why I couldn't do more.
